question: Will the United States negotiate in the same way as China?
interview_answer: Respect for China. Respect for its leadership.

question: What other political means can you use to help the House of Representatives introduce bills?
interview_answer: We have an economic report, which shows that our economy would be stronger if we were to carry out immigration reform. We have evidence that our housing market would be stronger if migrants were in such a situation. If migrants pay fines and pay taxes, they are now able to truly enter the housing market. We have strong evidence that if we do immigration reform, our technology and research advantages will be better. We know that the Senate Bill strengthens border security, and on the unprecedented resources I have invested in border security, our economy will be stronger. So, if your main priority is border security, I think that our housing market will be stronger. If migrants are in such a situation, we'll be stronger. If migrants pay fines, then we'll be stronger. We know that the Senate Bill has a system of responsibility for hiring undocumented workers. That's what people are saying. I agree. That the system of holding employers accountable is in place. That's what I'm saying, that's what I's saying.

question: Do you still think Putin's last wish was the cold war?
interview_answer: The answer is, I think he still doesn't want any full-blown war, first. Secondly, I think he's dealing with what he thinks was the most tragic thing that happened to Russian mothers. Yes, I think he will. But I think he's going to pay a serious and valuable price for it, and he's not going to pay for it now. I think he's going to regret it. I think he still doesn't want any full-blown war -- I think I think I can talk about it in a public forum. I think I can't be sure he's dealing with what he thinks is the most tragic thing that happened to Russian mothers. The Berlin Wall collapsed, the Empire was lost, the foreign country, etc. The conditions in the Soviet Union have split. I think he has eight time zones, a burning mystery, and it won't freeze again, and he has a lot of oil and gas, but he's trying to find his place in China and the West.

question: You still think Putin doesn't want the cold war?
interview_answer: The answer is that he still doesn't want a war, number one. Number two, I think he's gonna try the west, show the US and NATO as much as he can. Yeah, I think he's gonna do it, but I think he's gonna pay a big and expensive price for it, and he doesn't think it's gonna cost him right now, and I think he's gonna regret it. Now, if the Berlin Wall collapses, the empire is lost, the neighbouring country is gone, and so on. The Soviet Union splits up, but think about what it has. He's got eight-hour zones, a burning tunnel that won't freeze again, of course, a situation in which NATO has a lot of oil and gas, but he's trying to find his place in the world between China and the West.

question: What action do you intend to take over the next two years to change the people's perception of the direction of the country, especially as you consider running for the presidency in 2024?
interview_answer: Because they know what we're doing. The more they know what we're doing, the more they support it. Do you know that someone wants us to get rid of the changes we made in the price of prescription drugs and raise prices again? Do you know that someone wants us to get out of these roads and bridges and the Internet and so on? I don't know anything -- I think the problem is that the main legislation we've passed -- and some of these bipartisan attacks -- takes time to be recognized. For example, you already have over $1 trillion in infrastructure funding, but not many spades are put on the ground. This takes time -- for example, I congratulate a California on the phone, and then someone is...

question: What does he intend to do differently over the next two years in order to change people ' s attitude towards the country ' s leadership, in particular by providing a presidential candidate in 2024?
interview_answer: There's nothing, because they're just trying to figure out what we're doing. How much more do they know about what we're doing, there's more support? You know someone who wants to get rid of the changes we've made in the prices of prescribed drugs, and what we're gonna raise again? You know someone who wants us to get away from building these roads, bridges and the Internet, etc. I don't know anything. I think the problem is that the basic laws that we're adopting, and some of them are bi-party, take time to get recognition. For example, you have more than a billion dollars in infrastructure money, but not so many words on earth.

question: Is ASEAN ready to play the most active role in world affairs that the United States would like to play?
interview_answer: I was not outside the city, so I look only at the press releases, I did not have a full briefing on Israel ' s intentions and what it has told our administration, but such activities will never be useful when it comes to peace talks, and I am concerned that we do not see each side making additional efforts to achieve progress in the region, which could ultimately create a framework for a safe country, Israel, living side by side and side in peace with sovereign Palestine, but we will continue to work in Asia because it is in the interests of the world, is in the interests of the people of Israel and is in the interest of the Palestinian people to reach such an agreement, and we want China to achieve success and prosperity for all of us.

question: Are you satisfied with Vice President Harris' voting rights?
interview_answer: Yeah, yeah, right.

question: How does China see its place and role in international affairs?
interview_answer: Thanks to your questions, China and the United States need and can cooperate in a wide range of areas. As the international situation continues to undergo complex, volatile and profound changes, the areas in which our countries need to work together will be more stable.

question: How does China view its own position and role in international affairs as they develop?
interview_answer: In recent years, China and the United States have worked together to deal with the consequences of the global financial crisis, working together to solve and resolve some regional problems, such as Korea ' s nuclear issue, Iran ' s nuclear issue, Syria ' s situation, etc. We have also joined forces to address some serious issues, such as climate change, terrorism and the Ebola epidemic.

question: Do you still think Al-Qaeda has been eliminated?
interview_answer: As I said in my May speech at the National Defense University, the core of al-Qa`idah, which I mentioned in May, was destroyed behind the same defense university lecture, and their capabilities have not been destroyed. But I also said that al-Qa`idah and other extremists have spread to regional groups that may pose significant risks. I would like to remind you that, as early as May, I wanted to remind you that the Al-Qa`idah group, which was so intense and relatively concentrated, attacked our organization on 11 September, had collapsed and had little operational capacity.

question: Do you still think Al-Qaida was destroyed?
interview_answer: What I said in the same speech by the National Defense University in May, which I mentioned earlier, is that Aida's core is in his heels, but I also said that Aida and other extremists have become regional groups that can create significant dangers, and I refer to this speech in May, where I specifically said that, while they are less inclined to launch impressive attacks on a country like 9/11, they can follow our embassies, they can follow our business, they can be destabilizing and dispersive in countries where the security system is weak, and that's exactly what we see now, so we still have regional organizations like AARP that can pose a threat that can potentially bring a bomb on the Embassy wall and kill some people, and that requires that we can strengthen operational capacity and that we can say that it is much stronger.

question: Did the president try to prevent or allow them access to his tax returns?
interview_answer: They're being audited, as I told you. They've been audited for a long time. They're very complicated. They won't understand them. They won't understand them. They're done by the country's largest and best law firm. The accounting firm, too. The accountants are very, very large and powerful from a respectful point of view. They're a big firm. They're very respected, and they're a great law firm. You know that. They've done these things. They've put them in. They don't understand tax returns. Now, they don't understand. Now, I've made over 100 pages of declarations, and I believe that's in the office. When people go to and see them, they're very disappointed. They've seen this -- you know, the details. You've said more from this point of view.

question: If the president tries to block him or let him get tax returns,
interview_answer: Well, listen, as I was saying, they were under audit, they were long-term very complex, they were extremely complex, they wouldn't understand, they were created among the largest and best firms in the country, they were made from the largest and best accounting firms, accounting -- it's a very big, powerful firm in terms of respect, they're very respected, they're a big firm, they're a big law firm, or they know it very well, they do it, they do it, but people don't understand the tax returns, I think now I'm making over a hundred pages in offices, and when people see this presentation and see its scale, they're very disappointed, and they saw that you know that you'd get a lot more, and I think we've recorded it three times, but it's a very big company, but you get no more than you can get from taxes, but you don't understand it.

question: Two leaders were asked about their views on the future direction of climate change.
interview_answer: First, we insist on moving every one of our industries to one place, so that they can take advantage of cheaper, broader and more renewable sources of energy, be it solar or wind, hydrogen or green hydrogen. We've been doing a variety of things. Moreover, we find ourselves on a very serious planet. We've been doing a lot of things because of protection. In addition, we're on a very bad track, and we're on a very bad track, and we're not on a much cheaper track, and we've been working on a huge energy project.

question: To ask both leaders for their views on how to proceed with climate change.
interview_answer: First, I believe that this is an existential threat to humanity: climate change is the most serious challenge we face as human beings; we need to keep it below 1.5 degrees Celsius; we have made tremendous progress here in the United States in addressing this problem, doing three things: first, I insisted that we move our industry into a position where it can use the cheapest, most extensive and affordable renewable energy sources, whether solar or wind or hydrogen, and green hydrogen; we are working on a number of things; and we are in a situation where we have reduced renewable energy sources, for example, I have committed to making 30 per cent of all our lands and oceans underdeveloped by 2030.

question: Can you assure the American people that you don't want to profit from the presidency?
interview_answer: So I'll tell you: I've spent — I've spent a lot of time — and I think I'm going to spend a lot of time — in the combination of loss and opportunity — and maybe I'm going to spend three billion to five billion dollars on the presidency. The only thing I care about is this country. Otherwise, I don't care. I won't do it. People ask me how much you think it's going to cost. Between opportunity and non-doing, I've spent a lot of money on speaking. Now I've always spoken, you know what I got? Zippo. That's good. I've done a lot of great work and I don't do that much.

question: What guarantees, if any, can you give to the American people who do not seek to benefit from the presidency?
interview_answer: Well, I'll tell you what I've spent on this, and I think that in a combination of losses and opportunities, maybe I'll cost between $3 billion and $5 billion to be president, and the only thing I care about is this country, I wouldn't worry, otherwise I wouldn't, people asked me what you think it is, and between the opportunity not to do things, I've got a lot of money to make speeches, now I'm making speeches all the time, you know what I have, Zippo, and that's good, and I've done a lot of big jobs and big deals that I don't do anymore, this city, this is a great place, because the things I do for the country, and this is much more important for me.

question: How long can relations between Greece and foreign financial institutions last?
interview_answer: But I do believe that one lesson we are trying to apply is that structural reforms and good fiscal management must be combined with growth strategies. Not only for a number of reasons, you cannot fully compare the size of the United States with that of Greece.

question: How far can relations between Greece and foreign financial institutions go?
interview_answer: Well, you're right that the economy can't be completely compared between the United States and Greece for a number of reasons, not only because of the size of the economy, but also because we've suffered a very serious fall, we lost 800,000 jobs a month when I arrived at the office. In fact, the economy was falling faster than it was during the Great Depression, but we were able to intervene, apply lessons learned, stabilize and then start to grow again. But I think one of the lessons we're trying to do is that it's important to combine structural reforms and good fiscal management with growth strategies, because when their economy is growing and income is growing, it helps to ease the debt burden, and sometimes, if their only approach is to cut spending at a time when the economy is getting back on, the economy is getting even worse, and maybe we can add debt.

question: Requesting information about Holder.
interview_answer: Mr. Prime Minister, forgive me that you are right, and I am fully convinced that Eric Holder is the Attorney-General. He is an outstanding Attorney-General, and his work is just, and I expect him to continue to do so. Okay? You are talking about the empty side of the cup, and I like to look at things half full of glass instead of half empty. We want to see the sensitivity of the international community to what is happening. That is what we in Turkey are trying to do, and I believe the United States. There are other countries, the United Nations Security Council, the Arab League, and others — although not part of them — that are still sensitive to what is happening. Our goal is to speed up this process, and I will also visit other countries — my Foreign Minister will visit others — to see how we can speed things up, prevent more deaths and ensure a transition to democracy. Our goal is to see dictatorship, and to make all democracies believe in this democracy.

question: Requesting information on Holder.
interview_answer: Mr. Prime Minister, you're right, I completely trust Eric Holder as Attorney General, he's an exceptional Attorney General, and he's doing his job in good faith, and I hope he'll keep doing it. You're talking about the empty part of the glass. I like to see things with a middle glass, not half empty. We want to see the international community view what's happening now, and that's what we're fighting like Turkey, and I think the United States is doing the same.

question: What guarantees do you have that the F-16 will not escalate this war?
interview_answer: I have received a clear guarantee from Zelenski — they will not — that they will not use it to go to Russian territory into the Russian geographical territory, but they will be able to do so as long as Russian forces are present in Ukraine in the region.

question: What guarantees do you have that the F-16 supply will not escalate this war?
interview_answer: I am absolutely certain that Zelensky will not use it to infiltrate Russian geographical territory, but wherever Russian troops are inside Ukraine in this area, they could do so.

question: If you see Kim Jong-un tomorrow in the demilitarized zone, will you cross the border into North Korea?
interview_answer: I will, I will, I will, I will. I will. I will. I will. I'll feel it was easy to do it. We'll talk about China. We basically agreed that we were going to continue the negotiations — I've just ended it for a while — we're going to continue the negotiations. We agreed that I wouldn't pay $325 billion in tariffs, if I wanted to; I said, where you know we wanted to see it, we could sell a lot of things. I said, we could keep selling them. If these were Chinese companies, they really wanted to sell a lot of other things. We talked about it. We basically didn't. We said, we didn't go to save them until it was over. We had to see. The thing I allowed was -- many people were surprised — we just keep doing it, we just keep doing it, we just keep doing it, and we just keep doing it.

question: If you meet Kim Jong-un in the demilitarized zone tomorrow, will you cross the border into North Korea?
interview_answer: As far as China is concerned, we basically agreed that we would continue the negotiations that I completed some time ago and continue the negotiations. We agreed that we would not charge the $325 billion that I could charge, if you wish; where you know that we have made enough progress depending on where you want to look and where you want to start. Very advanced. We are talking about many other things.

question: Information to President Moon on inter-Korean cooperation and sanctions.
interview_answer: I really like the president of the moon, and we have a very good relationship. Believe it or not, I have a very good relationship with almost all the leaders.

question: No meeting with Jacob Black's family.
interview_answer: Well, I talked to the priest — a great man — the priest at home. I think it's best not to do anything in a place where lawyers are involved. They want me to speak, but they want lawyers to be involved, and I think it's inappropriate, so I didn't. But I did talk to the priest at home, and he was a good man, a great man. I think we had a great conversation. I might have, you know, do that at some point. But they did have a lawyer who wanted to speak on the phone, and I said, no, it was not appropriate, but I just said hello to the priest. But I did talk to the priest. Yes, please.

question: Don't date Jacob Blake's family.
interview_answer: Well, I talked to the pastor, the wonderful man, the family pastor, and I thought it would be better if I didn't do anything where the lawyers were involved, but they wanted me to talk to the lawyers, and I thought it was inappropriate, so I didn't do it, but I talked to the pastor of the family, who is a good person, a wonderful person, and I thought we were really good at talking, and at some point I could do it, but they had a lawyer who wanted to be on the phone, and I said no, it was inappropriate, but I just gave my best wishes, but I talked to the pastor again.

question: Were the White House involved in these negotiations?
interview_answer: Yes, we've been involved in it, and it's a short-term agreement, because we think that, ultimately, the block grants to the state will be an answer, and that would be a very good solution. We think that this solution will not only save money, but also improve people's health care with a very, very small premium, and you'll see that we either have a vote, or we're very close to getting a vote, and we'll get a vote, because we do have the potential to have huge health care in our country, so they're actually working, but this is a short-term solution, so we won't have this very dangerous time, and they're approaching a short-term solution, which will last one or about two years, because we have -- you know, we have a vote or we're very close to getting a vote.

question: Did the White House participate in these negotiations?
interview_answer: Yes, we were involved, and this is a short-term agreement, because we think that eventually the subsidies of the blocs that will come into the state will be the answer. It's a very good solution. We believe that this not only saves money, but will also give people a lot more health care with a very small, very small one, and you look at what happened to it, and you're also calculated much lower so that they can use Lamar very, very hard working with the Democrats on the other hand, and Patty Murray is one of them, especially and they are approaching the short-term solution, and this decision will be about a year or two years from now, and it will overtake us because we have, you know, we have voices or we are very close to getting votes, and we will get votes, because we really have the potential to have more medical assistance for our country.

question: What's the big deal about calling the president of Taiwan?
interview_answer: I think that all of our foreign policy should have a new vision. I think that I think that all of our foreign policy should have a new vision. I think — I said it before — I'm very proud of what I did. I think I am a better president now, and I think it is better than at first. But if you have been in the bubble for eight years, you start to see something that you benefit from — the benefits of democracy, the United States benefit from new perspectives. I think it is not just the privilege, but the duty of a new president to review everything that the United Nations does, and to see what is wise and what is not. I think I said — I'm very proud — when I come in, I assume that any new president will do the same thing. Given the importance of the relationship between the United States and China, it is possible that China will play a greater role in international affairs from the world economy, national security, our presence in other countries in Asia — perhaps without bilateral relations, and see what is better, and if I do it in a normal way, then I would do a much worse thing on the path of the presidency.

question: What's the problem with a short phone call with Taiwan's president?
interview_answer: I think I'm very proud of the work that I've done, and I think I'm better than the president now when I started, but if you're here eight years in a bubble, you start seeing things somehow, and you win it, democracy wins, the US benefits from some new prospects, and I think it should be not only a prerogative, but also the responsibility of the new president to look at everything that's been done and see what makes sense and what doesn't. That's what I did when I got into the world economy, national security, our presence in Asia and the Pacific politics, China just needs to do this exercise, and given the importance of the relationship between the US and China, given that the world economy is at stake, what it deserves, the only thing that matters is that the government has to do all the time you have.

question: What are the views of the President on the prospect of a major tax bill for his second term in office in the light of the decision to appoint Senator Max Fox as Ambassador to China?
interview_answer: It says that Max Baukes will be an outstanding ambassador to China, and I hope to be confirmed as soon as possible. My expectation and hope is that if the Senate Democrats — or the House of Representatives and Senate Democrats and Republicans — are serious about tax reform, it will not depend on one person, and it will depend on all of us working together. My office is ready, willing and eager to engage with both sides and engage in dialogue on how to simplify tax laws, make them more equitable, make them generate more jobs and do the right thing for middle-class Americans.

question: Are you realistic about tactics and possibilities?
interview_answer: Thank you for not having changed in the new conference room. Yes. I mean, as I told you last November, I was part of a group of Americans who were not in favour of what happened in Iraq, because it seemed that everything we did was doomed to fail. In other words, sectarian violence is rampant. I had a choice, as some suggested, to withdraw, to hope that the chaos and violence that might occur in the capital would not spread to all parts of the country, or to send more troops to prevent unrest and violence from happening in the first place. That was my decision. So, it was a realistic assessment, because I thought that all the efforts we made in Iraq were doomed to fail. In other words, the lesson was that people would not be satisfied with getting America out of Iraq. The reason Al-Qaida wanted to harm us here was the people here. That was their purpose. What they wanted to do was to make things happen to me, and what they wanted to do was innocent. I made a hard assessment. That was the ideology of Iraq, if we failed in Iraq, so did the Americans.

question: Were you realistic about strategy and what's possible?
interview_answer: Well, thank you for the follow-up work, nothing has changed in the new room, anyway, yes, I mean, as I said last November, right now, I was part of a group of Americans who didn't approve of what was happening in Iraq, because it seemed that all the efforts that we had brought about at this point were about to fail, in other words, interfaith violence was really furious, and I had to make a decision, and it was a realistic evaluation, as some suggested, and I hope that the chaos and violence that could happen in the capital would not spill throughout the country and send more troops to prevent chaos and violence first and foremost, and this decision that I made, so it was a realistic evaluation, on its own.

question: Do you have a single policy that you'll keep in place?
interview_answer: Not too much. I must say, the opposite side of the spectrum.

question: Is there one policy you'd keep in her place?
interview_answer: Well, not too much, I have to say, it's the opposite side of the spectrum.

question: Does the Russian Government have any compromising material about President Trump or his family?
interview_answer: [] President Trump and good, President Trump's position on Crimea is well known, and he remains firm in it. He continued to claim that he was illegal. We... hmm. We held a referendum in strict conformity with the Charter of the United Nations and international law. For us, this is a matter [indistinct], and now it concerns compromising material. Yes, I heard these rumours that we allegedly collected compromising material about Mr. Trump when he visited Moscow. Now, distinguished colleague, let me tell you, when President Trump visited Moscow at that time, I didn't even know that he was in Moscow. I treated President Trump with full respect, but while he was a private person, a businessman, no one told me that he was in Moscow.

question: Can a new cold war be avoided with China?
interview_answer: In answer to the first part of your question, I am absolutely convinced that there is a need here — not a new cold war. We — I met with Xi many times, and we were frank and clear. I think that China has no immediate attempt to invade Taiwan. I made it clear that our policy in Taiwan has not changed. That is the same position we took. I made it clear that we wanted a peaceful solution to the cross-Strait problem. So we never did. I am sure that he fully understood what I said. I think he understood what he said. I think that the United States is better prepared than any country in the world, both economically and politically, to respond to the changing world environment. I think Xi is — we agree that we will create an environment for those details that we have to resolve further. We agree that our Chief of Staff — our proper cabinet members and others are sitting together with another country — all the details that we have given — are involved in any detail about Kin.

question: Can a new cold war with China be avoided?
interview_answer: Well, to answer the first part of your question, I am absolutely certain that there is no need for a new cold war. " We " have met with Xi Jinping repeatedly, and we have been frank and clear with each other on all fronts, and I do not think that there are any inevitable attempts by China to invade Taiwan, and I have made it clear that our Taiwan policy has not changed. I have made it clear that we want to resolve the intersection peacefully, and therefore we must never approach it, and I am convinced that he has the correct understanding of what he has said, and I understand, and I think that the United States is better than any other country in the world, economic or political, to cope with changing circumstances in the world, and I think that Xi Jinping now agrees that we will create a number of circumstances in which we should address more detail that we need our Head of Cabinet and other members who sit down and discuss with this press.

question: Pushing the returns plan.
interview_answer: See, symbolically, this is important. I will be the first to visit the President of the United States in Laos. When you consider the history of the United States and Laos, I think it would be useful to see what has happened in the evolution of our relations with Viet Nam. I have just visited Viet Nam. First, when we try to build trust, much can be done about the legacy of war. For Laos, it is about dealing with unexploded ordnance, which still haunts a large rural area. And since Laos are still a relatively poor country developing, their ability to clean up these histories alone is hampered by a lack of resources. I think it is very useful to see what has happened in the evolution of our relations with Viet Nam. First, when we try to build trust, much can be done around the legacy of war.

question: Pressure plans in exchange
interview_answer: And when we think about the history of the United States and Laos, I think it would be useful to see what has happened in the evolution of our relationship with Viet Nam, the country I have just visited, first, when we try to build confidence, we can do a lot to solve the problems of the legacy of war. Lao believes that this means that we are dealing with unexploded devices, which continue to cause great damage to the fields. Laos is still a relatively poor country that is developing its own capacity to clear itself of the scarcity of resources. " We have to help " . " I hope " that at our meetings we can provide real assistance within a few days that guarantees that innocent children working through the field or the farmer are trying to clarify a field or initiative that does not make sense for us.

question: What do you believe Vietnam can offer to facilitate negotiations between the United States and North Korea?
interview_answer: On this issue, Viet Nam is committed to scrupulous compliance with all relevant United Nations Security Council resolutions, and we support the denuclearization of the Korean peninsula and will do our utmost to contribute to the denuclearization of the Korean peninsula.

question: What do you think Viet Nam could bring to the negotiating table between the United States and North Korea?
interview_answer: In this regard, Viet Nam pledges to take all relevant United Nations Security Council resolutions seriously, and we support the denuclearization of the Korean peninsula, and we will do our utmost to contribute to the denuclearization of the Korean peninsula.

question: Are they continuing to develop their own programmes?
interview_answer: He says the tests won't start. He says he won't test rockets, missiles or anything related to nuclear weapons. I can only tell you what he's telling the truth. We'll see. Yes, please, continue behind, red, red, red.

question: Do they continue their program?
interview_answer: Said the tests wouldn't start, said there wouldn't be any tests for missiles, missiles or anything related to nuclear weapons, and all I can say is what he said, and we'll see.

question: Was there a problem of abduction?
interview_answer: Yes.

question: In the absence of a significant improvement in the human rights situation, do you consider lifting the sanctions?
interview_answer: No, I'm going to make a big improvement. I want to know that it won't happen. Again, once you start this process, there's a point, even if you don't finish it for a while, because it can't happen in science or mechanically, but you can't go back. You know, once we get to that point, I'm going to start thinking. Yeah. Go on. Go on. Go on. Go on.

question: Did it ever consider lifting sanctions without significantly improving the human rights situation?
interview_answer: And once you start this process, there will be a time when, even if you haven't finished for a while, because it can't happen from a scientific or mechanical point of view, but you can't go back.

question: How did you react to Scooter Libby's defense team about calling Vice President Cheney to testify in the CIA leak?
interview_answer: What I read in the paper today is an interesting story. This is an ongoing case that I'm going to comment on. I think it's interesting.

question: Will you lose faith in that?
interview_answer: No, I think he'll do it, and I really believe it. Otherwise, I wouldn't do it. I really believe it. It's really an engine test station, except for all the others that they agreed to do. It's -- they have a very powerful engine test station, because we can see that because of the heat of this site -- it's released out -- and it's great to see it. Yes, I can -- I'm very happy. I'll tell you -- I'm very happy with the two points you mentioned -- but I think you might mean something that isn't at the engine test site. I think -- I think -- I think -- he'll probably do these things. I'm probably wrong. I mean, I might stand in front of you for six months and say, "Hey, I'm wrong." I know I'm never gonna admit it, but I'll find one -- I can find an excuse.

question: Will you lose faith in this process?
interview_answer: No, I think he really will, otherwise I wouldn't, and I really believe that, and it was an engine test object, besides all the other things that you agreed to do, it was him, they have an object to test very powerful engines that we can see again because of the heat they're transmitting, and yes, I can... I'm very happy, I'll tell you that I'm very happy with these two points you mentioned, but I think you can say that this isn't a place to test engines, I think that he can honestly, I think I can be wrong, I can be in front of you in six months, and I'm wrong, I don't know that I admit it, but I'll find some excuse.

question: Are you worried that you're standing here today, that the American people might face a credibility gap?

question: What do you expect from your legacy when you return to the presidency and consider race relations?
interview_answer: More importantly, I hope that my voice tries to get Americans to understand the thorny ethnic legacy, to encourage people to listen to each other; to recognize that slavery and Jim Crow, as well as the legacy of discrimination, will not suddenly disappear with the passage of the bill on civil rights or the bill on voting rights or with the election of Barack Obama; that things have become better – indeed better – but that we have done more; and that, like the police task force that we have set up, my voice tries to encourage people to come up with practical, concrete solutions, if not the elimination of racial prejudice; that if my voice is real and positive, then I hope that it will not change everything, but it will surface; that it will enable us to struggle with these issues and say more work; that, like the police task force that we have set up, I will try to find more practical solutions; and that this view can be reduced – if not the elimination of racial prejudice. If my voice is real and positive, then I hope that it will not change everything; that it will affect them; that it will be equal to us, and that it is just to think about it.

question: What do you hope it will be your legacy when you look back at the presidency and think about race relations?
interview_answer: But what I can do is to answer your question, so to say how I tried to lead the country on this issue; I hope that my voice tried to make us all, as Americans, understand the complex heritage of race; to encourage people to listen to each other; to recognize that the legacy of slavery and Jim Crowe and discrimination has not suddenly disappeared with the adoption of the Civil Rights Act or the right to vote Act, or the election of Barack Obama; that the situation has improved, but that much more work remains to be done; and that, as with the Police Task Force that we have set up, I have tried to encourage people to come up with practical and concrete solutions that could reduce, if not eliminate, racial prejudice; and that if my voice was real and positive, I hope that the police will fail to deal with all of this, but with specific problems that can, if not address, the problems of racial prejudice; and if my voice was right, and this question could be more real.

interview_answer: Well, in fact, my position hasn't changed when I talk to the lawyers, they've been interested in me, through executive action, double the legislation that's stalled in Congress, and get a comprehensive approach, as provided for in Senate law, for example, it goes beyond my legitimate authorities, there are some things I can't do, there's some limitations on what falls within the scope of fiscal discretion in terms of how to apply existing immigration laws, and what we continue to do is talk to the Legal Office, which is responsible for telling us about the rules, the scope of our operations and where we should say that we don't deport 11 million people, on the other hand, we have serious resources at the border not now to detain people, but to blame and have enough taxes, etc. So we have to take the executive measures that I can't do in the legal sphere, so I can do the same with our legislation.

question: What will you send him today?
interview_answer: With regard to North Korea, South Korea and the United States, as well as the international community, we should all accept that the ultimate goal is for North Korea to abandon its nuclear weapons and promote North Korea to become a responsible member of the international community, which is in the interest of peace on the Korean peninsula and the world, as well as for North Korea's own development. I believe that, in order to encourage North Korea to follow this path and to change its views, we must all work together. In this regard, China may play a very broad role, so China may have a very broad influence, and therefore China's participation in these efforts is very important. With regard to China's influence, China is abandoning its nuclear weapons and promoting it to become a responsible member of the international community. With regard to the position of China and Russia, let alone the international community, I believe that China and Russia are pushing the prospects for the denuclearization of the Korean peninsula to the international community and are working closely together to bring the North Korean people on the right path.

question: Presidential evaluation of North Korea ' s nuclear test potential

question: Given this issue, what are your views on the future of Afghanistan?
interview_answer: So, ma'am, the man -- I should say, I am pardoning a 14-year-old boy from the South Waziristan institution in Pakistan. His father sent him to an Islamic school for education because he could no longer send him to school because his mother had a heart condition and they had to pay to treat him. He sent the boy to an Islamic school, from where he disappeared. A few months later, his father heard that he had been arrested in Afghanistan, and then he came to Afghanistan. He saw a teenager suicide, not a legitimate minor boy used by terrorists to commit suicide and kill other innocent people. I think that was his right to send him to an Islamic school because he could no longer go to an Islamic school, because his mother had to give him a new chance and he had to send his children back to you. I'm glad you did. You got a lesson from there: those of our enemies, those who use children, those who think in their minds, those boys who were used by terrorists to kill themselves, and those who were forced to kill them. We should pass a message to them.

question: What do you think of Afghanistan's future in the face of this problem?
interview_answer: Well, madam, man, I must say that I was a 14-year-old boy from the South Pakistan Agency of Waziristan, who was sent by his father to his mother to get an education, because he couldn't afford to be in school anymore, because his mother had a heart condition, and they had to spend money on his treatment. By sending a child in a madrasa, he disappeared from there. In a few months his father heard that he had been arrested in Afghanistan and then came to Afghanistan, and seeing that it was a teenager, an innocent underage child who had been used by terrorists to kill himself and other innocent people, I felt that it was the right decision to sell him, give him a new chance at education and a new life, and send a message to his mother that his son would return to you.

question: Are you sure President Putin intends to invade or move into Ukraine?

question: This post is part of our special coverage Egypt Protests 2011.
interview_answer: First of all, I want to make it clear: Chelsea Manning has been sentenced to severe prison terms. So, I think ordinary people who want to disclose vital and confidential information will think that this time will not go unpunished. I think that when Chelsea Manning is serving her sentence, we won't get that impression. I think that, given that she went to trial, due process was enforced, that she was responsible for the crime, that she was given a very disproportionate sentence -- that she had already served a considerable amount of sentence compared to other leakers. So, she has served a considerable period of time, which makes it feel like she has been overworked, rather than pardoning her sentence. I feel very uncomfortable. I feel that when justice has been served, and when we are dealing with national security, we need some people who, if possible, have legitimate concerns about the actions of the Government, superiors or the institutions in which they work, who try to work through a fixed channel and take advantage of those whom you have heard. I realize that, and I also realize that when we are dealing with the whole of national security, we have a conflict.

question: Reconciliation of the commutation of Manning's sentence with Vicki Lix's involvement in the Russian burglary in last year's elections:
interview_answer: First, let's be clear: Chelsea Manning has served a harsh prison sentence, so the notion that the average person who was about to reveal vital information would think that he was going to go unpunished, I don't think he'd get the impression of the sentence that Chelsea Manning passed. That was my opinion, because there was a proper trial, she took responsibility for her crime, that her sentence was too disproportionate to what other filters had given her, and that it served a considerable amount of time, that it made sense to change and not forgive her sentence, and I'm very comfortable that justice was being done and that we still received a message that when it comes to our national security, where possible, we need people who may have legitimate concerns about the actions of the Government or its superiors or the institutions in which they work, that they try to work in the security sector, that we, and that in general we should work in the security sector.

question: (2) Does the commitment to enhance assistance to Iraq include an increase in United States military personnel?

So we wanted to vote soon, because I thought that we were doing the right thing, one of which ISIS had one, one of which ISIS had a good leadership strategy, one of which we had a good training, one of which we had a good local strategy, one of which we had a good local strategy, one of which we had a good local place, one of which we had a good local place, one of which had a good local strategy.

question: Is it correct that President Karzai accused the Pakistani intelligence services of the recent terrorist attacks on Afghanistan?
interview_answer: First, we will investigate his allegations, and we will cooperate with him to reach the bottom of his allegations. But I certainly hope that the Government will understand the dangers of the movement of extremists from parts of Pakistan into Afghanistan. It is disturbing to us, and it should be troublesome to Afghanistan. We have a common enemy. We have a common enemy. Al-Qaida is an ally. Al-Qaida is an ally. We hurt Al-Qaida. We hurt Al-Qaida in various parts of the world, including Pakistan. We will maintain pressure on our friends in Pakistan. We certainly hope that the Government will understand the dangers of the movement of extremists in their countries.

question: Is President Karzai correct in accusing Pakistani intelligence services of a recent terrorist attack on Afghanistan?
interview_answer: First, we will investigate and work with you to find out what you claim, but we have no doubt that some extremists are leaving Pakistan for Afghanistan. This is a source of concern to us, and it should be a source of concern to Afghanistan. We share the common enemy. It would be extremists who use violence to undermine democracy or to disrupt democracy. Al-Qaida is a place where we are causing serious damage to Al-Qaida, and we must do so -- we have brutally beaten them and wounded them all over the world, including in Pakistan, and we will continue to exert pressure on Al-Qaida with our friends from Pakistan. I hope that the Government understands the dangers of extremists who are moving in his country. I think that they are doing so. In fact, we will have the opportunity to continue to study this issue with the Prime Minister of Pakistan as a friend.

question: Concern was expressed about the ability of Afghan forces to respond to high-profile attacks in the capital.
interview_answer: Remember, downsizing has not started yet. So we understand that this is a dangerous place, where the Taliban are still active and similar events occur. The problem is that, as I said in my speech, the tide of war is shrinking. We have moved to a transitional phase. As we have seen in Iraq, where we have destroyed our forces, where the rest of our troops will be withdrawn by the end of this year, but where Iraq is able to maintain a democratic government, and where violence can be fought, that can happen. We can even imagine a similar approach in Iraq, but we can see a very dangerous place in such a war.

question: Concern over the ability of Afghan forces to respond to major attacks in the capital.
interview_answer: So what we understand is that this is a dangerous place, that the Taliban are still active, and that such events will happen sometimes: the question is, in terms of the general trend, is there a build-up of Afghan capacity? Kabul, for example, where there is a large proportion of the Afghan population as a whole that has been under the supervision of the Afghan forces for a long time, and they have done quite a good job; Kabul is much safer than it was, and the Afghan forces in Kabul are much safer than they were before; this does not mean that such events can happen, and this is likely to continue for some time; but, as I said in my speech, the military tide in Kabul has weakened, we have moved to the transition phase.

question: Have you read the New Yorker's report on the Red Cross, which considers interrogation plans in CIA detention facilities to be tantamount to torture?

I also made it clear that part of the delay was the unwillingness of some countries to take back those held there. In other words, in order to make it work, we had to have places to let them go. I don't know if you've noticed a Senate resolution, and everyone except the three senators said that we didn't want those prisoners to stay in the country. I don't know whether it was a 97-3 vote, but it was a 3-3 vote. In other words, part of the problem was how we dealt with some people there. You said nothing. First, we worked with others and sent them back. It was a rather urgent order. Many people probably didn't want killers to be among them, and many thought you were killers. Second, of course, we wanted to make sure that when we sent them back, they weren't part of the problem, but part of the problem, Peter, the question was what we did.

question: Have you read New Yorker's report on the Red Cross that the CIA interrogation program amounts to torture?
interview_answer: I said that it should be the goal of the nation to close Guantánamo, I also made it clear that part of the delay was related to the reluctance of some countries to return some of those who were detained there, in other words, to do so, we have to have these people come, I don't know if you noticed the Senate resolution the other day, where everyone except the three senators said that we don't want these prisoners to be in the country, I don't know if it was vote 97-3, but it was something under three votes, in other words, part of the subject, Peter, it's a practical question of what we're doing with people, and you're saying that nothing happened, I'm not opposed to what is a state, we're working with other countries to get people back, and again, it's a rather complicated order.

question: Despite the statement by Vice-President Biden that the campaign was open, did the campaign remain open?
interview_answer: Not only did I carry out the last elections, but I did not participate in the cause of predicting future elections — my work is fair. I will not be one of them, and I will not be without those who are happy to believe in future elections. With regard to Cuba, we are not the cause of regime change. Our job is to ensure that the Cuban people enjoy freedom, have the capacity to participate in and shape their own destiny and lives, and support civil society. No matter what we do within Cuba, there will be an evolution. In part, it will be an intergenerational legacy. If you hear President Castro's comments, many of his arguments refer to actions that have taken place before my birth, and part of the message that I convey here is the end of the cold war. We are also ensuring that the Cuban people enjoy freedom and the ability to participate in and shape their own destiny and lives and to support many issues in our world. We are also talking about nuclear issues with Cuba, not just about the attitude of the Cuban Government towards their people, but about regional problems and concerns.

question: Is the race still open despite Vice President Biden's statement that it is?
interview_answer: Not only should I lead my last choice, but I am not in the process of predicting future elections, it is your job to do so. [...] There is no shortage of people who would be happy to express their views on this issue, and I will not be one of them. In Cuba we do not change the regime; we are working to ensure that the Cuban people have the freedom and the opportunity to participate and shape their own destiny and their lives and to support civil society; and I will develop, whatever we do, within Cuba.

question: Are you sure President Putin intends to invade or move into Ukraine?
interview_answer: The only thing I have any confidence in is that the decision is complete, complete, complete, complete, and complete Putin's. But I don't know whether he decided to do it. Now, in three meetings, no one will influence it. He's doing it. He's making it. I doubt what he's going to do on which side of the bed he's going to do in the morning. I think it's not irrational if he wants to talk about dealing with strategic doctrines and dealing with the structure of force in parts of Europe and Russia. But I don't know if he's going to do it. I don't know if he's going to do it. So far, three meetings we've held — OSCE and — in any case — have produced nothing, because the impression I got from the Secretary of State, the National Security Adviser and other high-ranking officials who are meeting is that they're going to talk about what he's going to do and what he's going to do. In the short term, I'm sure he's going to do what he's going to do — for example, he's going to do it to go back north.

question: Have you determined whether President Putin is planning to invade Ukraine or move to Ukraine, as you said?
interview_answer: Look, the only thing I know for sure is that the decision is entirely, completely, completely, only Putin's decision, no one else is going to make that decision, no one else is going to influence that decision, he's making that decision, and I suspect that it's important which side of the bed he's on this morning, exactly what he's going to do, and I don't think it's irrational, if he wants to talk about strategic doctrine and deal with the structures of power in Europe and Russia, but I don't know if he's decided to do it or not.

question: This post is part of our special coverage Egypt Protests 2011.
interview_answer: First of all, I want to make it clear: Chelsea Manning has been sentenced to severe prison terms. So, I think ordinary people who want to disclose vital and confidential information will think that this time will not go unpunished. I think that when Chelsea Manning is serving her sentence, we won't get that impression. I think that, given that she went to trial, due process was enforced, that she was responsible for the crime, that she was given a very disproportionate sentence -- that she had already served a considerable amount of sentence compared to other leakers. So, she has served a considerable period of time, which makes it feel like she has been overworked, rather than pardoning her sentence. I feel very uncomfortable. I feel that when justice has been served, and when we are dealing with national security, we need some people who, if possible, have legitimate concerns about the actions of the Government, superiors or the institutions in which they work, who try to work through a fixed channel and take advantage of those whom you have heard. I realize that, and I also realize that when we are dealing with the whole of national security, we have a conflict.

question: Reconciliation of the commutation of Manning's sentence with Vicki Lix's involvement in the Russian burglary in last year's elections:
interview_answer: First, let's be clear: Chelsea Manning has served a harsh prison sentence, so the notion that the average person who was about to reveal vital information would think that he was going to go unpunished, I don't think he'd get the impression of the sentence that Chelsea Manning passed. That was my opinion, because there was a proper trial, she took responsibility for her crime, that her sentence was too disproportionate to what other filters had given her, and that it served a considerable amount of time, that it made sense to change and not forgive her sentence, and I'm very comfortable that justice was being done and that we still received a message that when it comes to our national security, where possible, we need people who may have legitimate concerns about the actions of the Government or its superiors or the institutions in which they work, that they try to work in the security sector, that we, and that in general we should work in the security sector.

question: (2) Does the commitment to enhance assistance to Iraq include an increase in United States military personnel?
interview_answer: With regard to the latter issue, I did not intend to assume that they did, and I wanted to do, and I wanted to do it quickly, because ISIS, I think we could do it right. For ISIL, we have made great progress in pushing back ISIL from an area where they occupied or disrupted the local population, but we also saw areas like Ramadi, where they were displaced and then returned to another place. They were very flexible, they were not equipped, and they were also opportunistic. So we wanted to vote soon, because I thought that we were doing the right thing, one of which ISIS had one, one of which ISIS had a good leadership strategy, one of which we had a good training, one of which we had a good local strategy, one of which we had a good local strategy, one of which we had a good local place, one of which we had a good local place, one of which had a good local strategy.

question: (2) Does the promise to increase aid to Iraq include more US military personnel?
interview_answer: Well, on the last point, I'm not going to make a hypothesis about not doing this, and I'm going to do it, and hopefully we'll get a vote soon, because I think it's right. As far as ISIL is concerned, we've made significant progress in pushing ISIL out of the areas in which they occupied or destroyed the local population, but we've also seen areas like Ramadi, where they were moved to one place, and then re-into another place where they're fast and aggressive and opportunists, so one of the areas we need to improve is the speed in which we train Iraqi forces, where we train them directly and equipped them, and we have a position of training and assistance, they work effectively.

question: Is it correct that President Karzai accused the Pakistani intelligence services of the recent terrorist attacks on Afghanistan?
interview_answer: First, we will investigate his allegations, and we will cooperate with him to reach the bottom of his allegations. But I certainly hope that the Government will understand the dangers of the movement of extremists from parts of Pakistan into Afghanistan. It is disturbing to us, and it should be troublesome to Afghanistan. We have a common enemy. We have a common enemy. Al-Qaida is an ally. Al-Qaida is an ally. We hurt Al-Qaida. We hurt Al-Qaida in various parts of the world, including Pakistan. We will maintain pressure on our friends in Pakistan. We certainly hope that the Government will understand the dangers of the movement of extremists in their countries.

question: Is President Karzai correct in accusing Pakistani intelligence services of a recent terrorist attack on Afghanistan?
interview_answer: First, we will investigate and work with you to find out what you claim, but we have no doubt that some extremists are leaving Pakistan for Afghanistan. This is a source of concern to us, and it should be a source of concern to Afghanistan. We share the common enemy. It would be extremists who use violence to undermine democracy or to disrupt democracy. Al-Qaida is a place where we are causing serious damage to Al-Qaida, and we must do so -- we have brutally beaten them and wounded them all over the world, including in Pakistan, and we will continue to exert pressure on Al-Qaida with our friends from Pakistan. I hope that the Government understands the dangers of extremists who are moving in his country. I think that they are doing so. In fact, we will have the opportunity to continue to study this issue with the Prime Minister of Pakistan as a friend.

question: Concern was expressed about the ability of Afghan forces to respond to high-profile attacks in the capital.
interview_answer: Remember, downsizing has not started yet. So we understand that this is a dangerous place, where the Taliban are still active and similar events occur. The problem is that, as I said in my speech, the tide of war is shrinking. We have moved to a transitional phase. As we have seen in Iraq, where we have destroyed our forces, where the rest of our troops will be withdrawn by the end of this year, but where Iraq is able to maintain a democratic government, and where violence can be fought, that can happen. We can even imagine a similar approach in Iraq, but we can see a very dangerous place in such a war.

question: Concern over the ability of Afghan forces to respond to major attacks in the capital.
interview_answer: So what we understand is that this is a dangerous place, that the Taliban are still active, and that such events will happen sometimes: the question is, in terms of the general trend, is there a build-up of Afghan capacity? Kabul, for example, where there is a large proportion of the Afghan population as a whole that has been under the supervision of the Afghan forces for a long time, and they have done quite a good job; Kabul is much safer than it was, and the Afghan forces in Kabul are much safer than they were before; this does not mean that such events can happen, and this is likely to continue for some time; but, as I said in my speech, the military tide in Kabul has weakened, we have moved to the transition phase.

question: Didn't you attempt to undermine his intention to set up a meeting here in Washington?

question: Do you support Congressman Shaka Fata's bill, the American Debt Relief Act?

The recession is difficult for everyone, but obviously it is harder for those who earn less. I've been fascinated by what I've been doing since I came into office, the families that do the right thing every day, they're looking after their families, they're trying to keep up, they're just feeling that they're falling behind, they're just feeling that they're working hard, no matter how hard they're doing. Last week I got one -- her husband lost his job, hit the shop, and finally found a job. They think that work has been stable for months. Now they're looking back and trying to figure out how they're doing the second job. They're looking at them a little bit, and they think they're small, no matter how hard their work is. When I started doing business, we did a very stressful business, and we did a lot of things to stabilize.

question: Have you read the New Yorker's report on the Red Cross, which considers interrogation plans in CIA detention facilities to be tantamount to torture?

I don't know whether it was a 97-3 vote, but it was a 3-3 vote. In other words, part of the problem was how we dealt with some people there. You said nothing. First, we worked with others and sent them back. It was a rather urgent order. Many people probably didn't want killers to be among them, and many thought you were killers.
